CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and the two main contributions are stated early and kept distinct (phonetic LSTM; constraint-based generation). Writing quality is adequate and hyperparameters/limitations are reported, but some methodological descriptions (e.g. the transliteration/phonetic encoding pipeline and evaluation annotation procedures) are under-specified, and the notation around the constraint-satisfaction formulation could be more explicitly defined. Figures/tables and example poems are helpful where present, but the paper would benefit from a few more concrete examples and clearer description of human evaluation details. | B(4): The paper is generally well-organized and clearly lays out its two complementary methods early on (phonetic LSTM and constrained generative model), with experiments and evaluation metrics (syllabic misclassification, metrical accuracy, human judgments) following logically. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and adequately defined for the reader to follow, and the writing quality is described as adequate; figures, tables, and examples are implied to be informative though not highlighted as exceptional. A few presentation blemishes remain (e.g., discussion of transliteration/pronunciation limitations could be clearer and code release is not mentioned), but overall the paper communicates its ideas effectively for a systems-oriented audience.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → methods → experiments → conclusion) and the two main contributions are stated early and kept distinct (phonetic LSTM; constraint-based generation). Writing quality is adequate and hyperparameters/limitations are reported, but some methodological descriptions (e.g. the transliteration/phonetic encoding pipeline and evaluation annotation procedures) are under-specified, and the notation around the constraint-satisfaction formulation could be more explicitly defined. Figures/tables and example poems are helpful where present, but the paper would benefit from a few more concrete examples and clearer description of human evaluation details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is generally well-organized and clearly lays out its two complementary methods early on (phonetic LSTM and constrained generative model), with experiments and evaluation metrics (syllabic misclassification, metrical accuracy, human judgments) following logically. Notation and methodological descriptions appear consistent and adequately defined for the reader to follow, and the writing quality is described as adequate; figures, tables, and examples are implied to be informative though not highlighted as exceptional. A few presentation blemishes remain (e.g., discussion of transliteration/pronunciation limitations could be clearer and code release is not mentioned), but overall the paper communicates its ideas effectively for a systems-oriented audience.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares its models against a single unconstrained character baseline and reports multiple metrics and human judgments, but fails to include stronger and diverse baselines (e.g., non-neural constraint-based or classical metrical generation systems, or stronger neural baselines with explicit prosody modeling). It is unclear from the summary whether all comparisons were run under identical conditions (same data splits and evaluation protocols) and the analysis of differences—while present—does not exhaustively isolate contributions of the phonetic model versus the WFSM constraint module. Related work is said to be adequately covered, but the evaluation suite of baselines is incomplete, so comparisons are not fully comprehensive or convincing. | B(3): The paper includes a clear baseline (unconstrained character model) and multiple quantitative and large-scale human evaluations, which is useful, but it omits comparisons to other recent or specialized constrained-generation or phonetic-level poetry systems. While related work coverage is reported as adequate and the authors acknowledge some limitations, the experimental setup does not demonstrate fairness across a broader set of competitive baselines or ablations that would isolate the contributions of the phonetic encoding versus constraint machinery. From an applied-systems perspective this limits confidence that the proposed methods are meaningfully better than the state of the art.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares its models against a single unconstrained character baseline and reports multiple metrics and human judgments, but fails to include stronger and diverse baselines (e.g., non-neural constraint-based or classical metrical generation systems, or stronger neural baselines with explicit prosody modeling). It is unclear from the summary whether all comparisons were run under identical conditions (same data splits and evaluation protocols) and the analysis of differences—while present—does not exhaustively isolate contributions of the phonetic model versus the WFSM constraint module. Related work is said to be adequately covered, but the evaluation suite of baselines is incomplete, so comparisons are not fully comprehensive or convincing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper includes a clear baseline (unconstrained character model) and multiple quantitative and large-scale human evaluations, which is useful, but it omits comparisons to other recent or specialized constrained-generation or phonetic-level poetry systems. While related work coverage is reported as adequate and the authors acknowledge some limitations, the experimental setup does not demonstrate fairness across a broader set of competitive baselines or ablations that would isolate the contributions of the phonetic encoding versus constraint machinery. From an applied-systems perspective this limits confidence that the proposed methods are meaningfully better than the state of the art.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is on-topic and makes a meaningful system-level contribution with appropriate evaluation choices (including a large human study). I give a 4 because the work is a strong fit for the venue and shows practical impact, but outstanding issues noted by Reviewer A—limited theoretical analysis, sparse baselines/ablations, and missing details on statistical tests/inter-rater agreement and reproducibility—prevent a higher score until addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—neural generation of rhythmic verse using phonetic-level LSTMs and WFSM-constrained decoding—is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope and addresses the right audience (generative sequence models, constrained decoding). However, from the provided summary the paper lacks the kind of formal/theoretical analysis I expect (no argument or proof about why the methods should enforce metrical properties), compares to only a single unconstrained baseline (fewer than three substantive baselines/ablations), and reports human evaluation results without mention of inter-rater agreement or statistical testing; these omissions weaken its fit for a top-tier venue despite solid dataset and evaluation choices.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a strong fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it tackles an NLP generation task (automatic poetry) with novel, system-level contributions (phonetic-level LSTM and a hybrid generative + discriminative WFSA constraint solver) and reports a substantial human study (54% judged human) that demonstrates practical impact. The methods and evaluation (phonetic encoding, metrical metrics, large-scale human judgments) match community expectations, and the work will interest applied researchers exploring hybrid generative/discriminative systems. Minor concerns are the lack of mentioned reproducibility artifacts (code) and some dataset/pronunciation limitations, which should be addressed but do not preclude venue relevance.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper delivers a strong empirical evaluation with diverse automatic metrics and a large-scale human study, and that limitations (transliteration/pronunciation issues) and hyperparameters are reported. However, they also concur that the work lacks deeper ablation/sensitivity analyses, formal theoretical analysis, and clear code release for full reproducibility. Given the solid experimental contribution but incomplete analysis and reproducibility artifacts, a score of 4 reflects the paper’s substantive empirical value while acknowledging these important gaps.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments provide useful empirical evidence (large-scale human evaluation, multiple quantitative metrics, two distinct model architectures) but lack formal/theoretical analysis of why the methods should enforce poetic constraints (no proofs or bounds are reported), which from my theoretical perspective is a substantive omission. Ablation/sensitivity testing is limited (only an unconstrained character baseline is reported rather than multiple strong neural and non-neural baselines or comprehensive ablations of model components). Human evaluation reporting appears incomplete—no inter-rater agreement or significance testing is mentioned—while limitations are acknowledged (transliteration/pronunciation issues) and hyperparameters are reported, but code availability is not stated, hindering reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a solid empirical evaluation: multiple automatic metrics (metrical error counts, line-level accuracy, trigram similarity) plus a large-scale human evaluation showing 54% of machine poems judged human and one machine poem rated best, and it compares against a clear unconstrained baseline. It does discuss realistic failure modes (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary limits) and reports hyperparameters, and related work coverage is adequate. Missing, however, are explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses isolating contributions of key components (e.g., phonetic LM vs. transliteration errors, or the impact of different constraint weights), and reproducibility is weakened by no mention of released code or full pipeline artifacts. Given these strengths and the noted gaps, the experimental depth is strong but not exhaustive.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an interesting, practically relevant problem and presents methods (phonetic LSTM and constrained WFSM generation) with compelling human-evaluation results suggesting real-world potential. However, limitations—lack of formal analysis, sparse ablations/baselines, missing statistical details for the human study, and reproducibility concerns—temper confidence that it will substantially reshape the field. Taken together, the work is of moderate impact: likely to influence practitioners and open follow-on work but not to constitute a major theoretical advance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a clearly interesting problem (automatic rhythmic verse) and introduces two practical methods (phonetic LSTM and constrained generation via a discriminative WFSM) with a large-scale human judgment showing 54% perceived as human. However, from the provided summary there is no formal or theoretical analysis explaining why the methods should enforce prosodic constraints (I must penalize absence of formal argumentation), ablation/baseline comparisons are limited (only an unconstrained character model reported), and the human evaluation lacks reported inter-rater agreement/statistical testing. These limitations reduce confidence that the approach will substantially reshape research practice beyond the immediate niche, though it does suggest productive engineering directions for further work.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles a clearly interesting and practically relevant problem—automating rhythmic verse—using two practically-minded methods (phonetic-level LSTM and a generative+discriminative WFSM constraint approach) and reports a large-scale human evaluation where machine poems were judged human 54% of the time and one machine poem was rated best. The hybrid constraint-satisfaction formulation and phonetic encoding are likely to influence how practitioners approach controllable and form-aware generation beyond poetry, and the strong human-centric evaluation increases confidence in real-world applicability. Limitations (transliteration/OOV errors, pronunciation-dictionary issues) and the absence of explicitly mentioned code temper immediate reuse, but the work nevertheless opens meaningful follow-on directions for constrained neural generation and evaluation.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims and provides empirical evidence (phonetic LSTM, constrained generation, and a large human evaluation) but is weakened by limited baselines, missing statistical reporting and reproducibility details, and lack of theoretical analysis. Given the suggestive but methodologically incomplete support, a middle score reflects that the contributions are promising yet insufficiently validated to merit a higher soundness rating.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents empirical evidence for its claims (phonetic LSTM; constrained generation) but lacks any formal or theoretical analysis explaining why these methods should capture prosodic form (no proofs or bounds), which I penalize heavily. Experimental methodology is weak: comparisons are limited (only an unconstrained character baseline; fewer than three ablations/baselines) and key details of the human evaluation are missing (no report of inter-rater agreement or statistical significance), undermining the claim that machine poems are judged human 54% of the time. Some limitations are acknowledged (transliteration/pronunciation issues), but the logical gap between claimed generality (arbitrary forms/themes) and the presented constrained experiments is not adequately supported. Overall, the empirical results are suggestive but methodological gaps and missing analyses make the technical claims insufficiently justified.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear main claims and provides empirical evidence (phonetic LSTM results, constrained-generation WFSM approach, and a large-scale human evaluation reporting 54% judged human), but several methodological gaps weaken full technical support. Key issues: limited baselines (only an unconstrained character model), lack of mentioned statistical testing for human judgments, and known error sources (transliteration/pronunciation-dictionary OOVs) that the authors themselves flag. Mathematical content appears minimal and correct for the methods described, but reproducibility is unclear (no code mentioned) and some assumptions about phonetic decoding and metrical evaluation remain under-justified. Overall the experiments support the primary claims but additional baselines, statistical analysis, and artifact release are needed for stronger technical validation.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper offers useful, clearly differentiated combinations of techniques (phonetic-level LSTM and a hybrid generative+FSA constraint mechanism) that advance practical poetry generation but do not introduce a fundamentally new theoretical paradigm. Given the non-trivial implementation and likely impact on practitioners, yet acknowledging the lack of formal analysis and that the ideas build on existing components, a score of 4 appropriately reflects solid originality without claiming breakthrough novelty.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes two concrete approaches — training an LSTM on phonetic encodings to capture rhyme/rhythm, and coupling a character-level generative model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine for constraint-satisfaction — which are interesting and differentiate the work from simple unconstrained character models. However, both ideas are largely sensible combinations of existing techniques (phonetic/transliteration representations, neural language models, and FSA-style constraint enforcement) rather than fundamentally new paradigms. Crucially, the summary contains no formal/theoretical analysis or bounds explaining why these constructions should reliably capture poetic form, which (per my standards) reduces the methodological novelty from notable to moderate.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes two distinct methodological innovations—a phonetic-level LSTM trained on phonetic encodings to learn meter/rhyme implicitly, and a hybrid generative character model constrained by a discriminative weighted finite-state machine for form enforcement—that represent a creative, practical system combination beyond routine baselines. While automated poetry generation is a well-studied task, these specific approaches (phonetic-level modeling plus a constraint-satisfaction hybrid) are clearly differentiated from prior work and non-trivial to implement. Given the large-scale human evaluation and claimed success (54% judged human), the techniques would likely inform practitioners, though the overall problem setting is not novel enough to merit the highest score.

main_claims: Propose two novel methodologies for automatic generation of rhythmic poetry: a phonetic-level neural language model and a constraint-satisfaction approach combining a generative neural model with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine.
A neural language model trained on a phonetic encoding can learn implicit representations of poetic form and devices such as rhyme, rhythm and alliteration.
A character-level generative neural model constrained by a discriminative weighted finite-state machine can produce coherent poetry with arbitrary forms and themes.
A large-scale extrinsic evaluation found participants judged machine-generated poems as human-written 54% of the time, and a machine-generated poem was rated the best among evaluated poems.
_summary.methodology: Two approaches: (1) train an LSTM neural language model on phonetic encodings of poetry (with transliteration to/from orthography) to learn form and content implicitly; (2) use a character-level generative neural language model for content combined with a discriminative weighted finite-state machine to enforce rhythmic/form constraints, treating generation as constraint satisfaction.
metrics: syllabic misclassification count (compared to loose Iambic Pentameter)
percentage of lines with zero metrical mistakes
limitations: A significant number of errors in the phonetic-level model are likely caused by transliteration mistakes due to phonetic encoding/decoding and out-of-vocabulary coverage issues.
Pronunciation dictionaries suffer limitations for constructing syllabic stress classifiers.
Authors note future work should investigate morpheme-based models to handle complex and rare words (implying current limitations with character-based models).

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in computational linguistics with a strong track record in formal language design and theoretical analysis of generative models.
traits: Penalises papers that lack formal/theoretical analysis of model properties (deducts 1–3 points if no proof, bound, or explicit formal argument is given about why the method should work).
Requires comprehensive ablation studies and explicit comparison to strong non-neural and neural baselines (deducts 1–2 points if fewer than 3 ablations/baselines are provided).
Penalises human evaluations that omit inter-rater agreement metrics and statistical significance testing (deducts 1–2 points if these are missing or inadequately reported).
Insists on clear discussion of limitations and failure modes, including ethical considerations for generated content (deducts 1 point if missing).
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on rigorous, formal justification and methodological completeness; generous with novelty only when accompanied by solid theoretical or ablation evidence.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Systems Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher at an industry NLP lab, with experience deploying creative language generation systems and running large-scale user studies.
traits: Favors papers that introduce novel tasks or practical system combinations (adds 1–3 points for clear task/architecture novelty and real-world applicability).
Rewards comprehensive, large-scale human evaluations and end-to-end demos (adds 1–2 points if study size and experimental detail enable meaningful conclusions).
Values reproducibility artifacts (code, models, demos) and gives +1 if such assets are provided or clearly promised in the appendix).
Gives credit for creative integration of generative and discriminative approaches (adds 1 point when a paper demonstrates an effective hybrid system).
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes empirical impact, usability, and convincing human-centered evaluation; tolerant of limited theory if there is strong practical evidence and artifacts.
